The most common modern Western answer to “what makes someone a Christian” is: belief. Believe that Jesus is Lord, believe he died for your sins, believe he rose from the dead — and you are a Christian. This view has deep Protestant roots and enormous popular influence. But it creates immediate problems. The Problem of Demons The Letter of James poses the problem directly: “You believe that God is one; you do well. Even the demons believe — and shudder!” (James 2:19). Demons have impeccably correct theological beliefs. They know Jesus is the Son of God — they declared it to his face (Mark 1:24; 5:7). Correct belief, by itself, is not what distinguishes a Christian from a demon. “What good is it, my brothers and

The English word “faith” has been flattened, in modern usage, to mean “belief without evidence” or “holding a religious opinion.” Neither meaning captures what the Greek New Testament word pistis (πíστις) actually meant in its first-century context. Greek (NT) πíστις (pistis) Trust, allegiance, faithfulness, loyalty. In political usage: the trust a citizen pledges to a ruler. The word implies active, lived commitment — not passive mental acceptance. Greek (NT) πιστεύω (pisteuō) To trust, to entrust oneself to, to be faithful toward. “Believing in” Jesus (John 3:16) uses this verb with eis (into) — meaning to cast yourself into his care, not merely to accept his existence. Hebrew (OT) אמונה (emunah) Faithfulness

secs.h: Creedal Christianity — What Do We Actually Believe?
secs.x: While Christianity is not merely belief, it is not without specific content either. The Christian faith has a definite shape — a body of conviction about who God is, who Jesus is, and what he has done. This shape was formally expressed in the great creeds of the early Church. Why Creeds Exist The creeds were not invented by theologians looking for precision. They were born in the crisis of heresy — specifically the challenge of teaching that threatened to dissolve the identity of Jesus Christ. Arianism taught that Jesus was a superior creature, not fully God. Gnosticism taught that the material world was evil and the Creator a lesser deity. Nestorianism divided Christ into two persons. Each heresy had its own v

In the Oriental Orthodox understanding, becoming a Christian is not merely a decision made in the mind — it is a rebirth effected in water and the Spirit. Baptism is not a symbol of something that already happened; it is the event in which new birth happens. “Jesus answered, ‘Truly, truly, I say to you, unless one is born of water and the Spirit, he cannot enter the kingdom of God.’” — John 3:5 Baptism in the New Testament Every conversion account in Acts includes baptism as its immediate consequence — not as an optional next step but as the moment of incorporation into Christ. Pentecost: “Repent and be baptized, every one of you, in the name of Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of your sins. And you will receiv

secs.h: The Church as Body of Christ
secs.x: One of the most common modern ideas is that you can be a Christian without belonging to a church — that institutional religion is separate from personal faith in Jesus. This idea would have been literally incomprehensible to the New Testament writers and every Church Father. The Body Metaphor Is Not a Metaphor When Paul calls the Church the “Body of Christ” (1 Corinthians 12; Romans 12; Ephesians 4–5; Colossians 1), he is not choosing a flattering image for a human organization. He is making a claim about ontology — about what the Church actually is . Christ is present in the world through his Body; the Church is the mode of Christ’s continued presence and action. To be “in Christ” (a phrase Paul uses over 150

The dominant Protestant understanding of salvation is forensic — a legal declaration. God, as judge, declares the sinner “not guilty” because Christ’s righteousness is imputed (credited) to the believer. You remain exactly the same person; your legal status changes. This view has its roots in Western medieval theology and was sharpened by Luther and Calvin. The Oriental Orthodox understanding — shared with Eastern Orthodoxy and patristic Christianity — is fundamentally different: salvation is transformation . The goal is not a change of legal status but a change of nature — participation in the divine life itself. Theosis — Deification The Greek word theosis (θέωσις) means “becoming God” or “deification.” This

The first word of Jesus’ public ministry in Matthew’s Gospel is metanoeite — “repent” (Matthew 4:17). And John the Baptist’s message was the same. Repentance is not the beginning and end of Christianity; it is the mode of the entire Christian life. Greek (NT) μετάνοια (metanoia) Literally “change of mind” — but in practice, a total reorientation of one’s whole being: mind, will, emotion, and action. Not guilt and self-punishment but a fundamental turning: away from the self-centered life and toward God. Latin (Vulgate) paenitentia The Latin translation of metanoia by Jerome. It means “penitence” or “doing penance” — a subtle but significant shift toward the performance of acts of satisfaction, rather than the i

secs.h: The Sacraments — Mysteries of the Kingdom
secs.x: The Oriental Orthodox Church calls the sacraments mysteries ( mysteriōn / mystirāt ) rather than “sacraments.” This name is deliberate: these are not merely ceremonies or symbolic rituals but genuine encounters with the divine — points where heaven and earth meet, where the invisible grace of God is conveyed through visible material means. The number of sacraments recognized varies — Coptic tradition typically lists seven; other Oriental churches have similar lists. The essential point is that the Christian life is sacramental : material reality — water, oil, bread, wine, hands — is taken up into the work of divine grace. Baptism New birth in water and the Spirit (John 3:5). Entry into the Body of Christ. Forgi

secs.h: Prayer, Fasting & Almsgiving — The Three Pillars
secs.x: In Matthew 6, Jesus assumes his disciples will practice three things: prayer, fasting, and almsgiving. He does not say “if you fast” but “when you fast.” He does not say “if you give to the needy” but “when you give to the needy.” These three practices — which Jesus gave detailed instructions for in the Sermon on the Mount — form the backbone of Christian spiritual life in the Oriental Orthodox tradition. I Prayer The Coptic Church prays seven times daily (the Agpeya / Liturgy of the Hours). Prayer is not a technique for getting things from God but a relationship — the ongoing conversation and communion with the living God. “Pray without ceasing” (1 Thess 5:17) is not hyperbole but a description of the saint’s

secs.h: Visible vs. Invisible Church — A Protestant Innovation
secs.x: A significant Protestant theological tradition — especially in the Reformed/Calvinist stream — distinguishes between the “visible church” (the institutional church with its sacraments and ordained ministry) and the “invisible church” (the true spiritual community of the elect, known only to God). The point was to explain how hypocrites and unregenerate people can be members of the institutional church without being truly saved. The Problem with This Distinction While the distinction addresses a genuine pastoral problem — not everyone who attends church is actually living a Christian life — the Oriental Orthodox tradition argues it introduces a fatal confusion. Once you establish an “invisible church” of truly s

secs.h: Nominal Christianity — Christian in Name Only
secs.x: The most serious challenge to the question “what is a Christian” is not theoretical but practical: millions of people call themselves Christians who show no evidence of the transformed life the New Testament describes. This is nominal Christianity — Christianity in name only. What the New Testament Says Jesus’ words in the Sermon on the Mount are unambiguous and alarming: “Not everyone who says to me, ‘Lord, Lord,’ will enter the kingdom of heaven, but the one who does the will of my Father who is in heaven” (Matthew 7:21). And: “On that day many will say to me, ‘Lord, Lord, did we not prophesy in your name, and cast out demons in your name, and do many mighty works in your name?’ And then will I declare to the

Drawing together the biblical and patristic tradition, here are the markers of authentic Christian life as understood in the Oriental Orthodox tradition. These are not entrance requirements to be achieved before becoming a Christian — they are the ongoing shape of a life being lived in Christ. 1 Baptized into Christ Has entered the Body of Christ through the waters of baptism. The new birth has been received, not merely aspired to. 2 Confesses the Creed Holds the apostolic faith: One God, Trinity, full divinity and humanity of Christ, his death and resurrection, the Church, bodily resurrection. 3 Belongs to the Church Is a member of the Body of Christ — not merely attending services as a cultural activity but g

secs.h: Church Fathers on What It Means to Be a Christian
secs.x: The Didache c. AD 80–120 The earliest non-canonical Christian text describes what a Christian does: “There are two ways, one of life and one of death, and there is a great difference between the two ways.” The Way of Life involves loving God and neighbor, serving the poor, confessing sins before the Eucharist, fasting on Wednesdays and Fridays, and praying the Lord’s Prayer three times daily. From the very beginning, Christian identity was practical and structured. St. Ignatius of Antioch d. c. AD 108 “Christianity is not a matter of persuasion, but of greatness, especially when it is hated by the world. Let us not merely call ourselves Christians, but be Christians.” ( Letter to the Magnesians 4). Writing on h

Can someone be saved without being baptized? The Oriental Orthodox tradition holds that baptism is the normative means of entry into salvation and the Body of Christ. However, the Fathers consistently recognized exceptions: the baptism of blood (martyrdom — dying for Christ before receiving water baptism) and the baptism of desire (sincere desire for baptism, dying before it is possible). These are not loopholes but recognitions that God is not bound by his sacraments even though we are. The Church does not limit God’s mercy; it points to the normal path God has established. “Truly, truly, I say to you, unless one is born of water and the Spirit, he cannot enter the kingdom of God.” — John 3:5 Are Protestants c

Pistis (πíστις) Greek NT word usually translated “faith.” Means trust, allegiance, faithful commitment — not merely intellectual belief. The faith that saves is active, entrusting, and loyalty-pledging. Metanoia (μετάνοια) “Repentance” — literally “change of mind/being.” A total reorientation of life toward God. Not guilt or self-punishment but genuine turning. The ongoing posture of the Christian life. Theosis “Deification” — the Eastern Christian understanding of salvation as genuine participation in the divine nature (2 Peter 1:4). The goal of the Christian life: becoming by grace what God is by nature. Ekklesia (εκκλησία) “Assembly, gathering.” The NT word for Church. Not a building or institution but an as

I. Introduction Few topics generate more heat — and less light — in contemporary Christian discourse than the role of women in the Church. On one side, progressive voices argue that the Church's traditional structures represent nothing more than the baptism of ancient patriarchy, awaiting correction by enlightened modernity. On the other side, reactionary voices sometimes reduce the question to a defense of hierarchy for its own sake, without adequate engagement with the dignity and indispensable ministry of women throughout the entire biblical and patristic tradition. The Oriental Orthodox position navigates a different path. It affirms with full conviction the complete equality of men and women in human digni

The subordination of women described in Genesis 3:16 ("your desire will be for your husband, and he will rule over you") is part of the curse — the disordered consequence of the Fall — not the original design of creation. The OO tradition, following Paul in Galatians 3:28 and the whole pattern of Christ's restoration, holds that redemption in Christ restores the original dignity of creation. The Church does not simply perpetuate the curse — it heals it.

III. Women in the Old Testament The Old Testament, despite its patriarchal cultural context, is remarkably populated with women who exercise leadership, prophecy, wisdom, and decisive agency in God's redemptive purposes. Miriam Prophetess · Exodus 15:20 Sister of Moses and Aaron, called a prophetess (neviah) and leader of Israel. Led the women of Israel in the Song of the Sea after the Exodus — the first recorded hymn of praise in Scripture. Micah 6:4 lists her alongside Moses and Aaron as one of the three leaders God gave Israel. Deborah Judge and Prophetess · Judges 4–5 The only female judge in Israel's history. She held court, settled disputes, received the word of the Lord, and led Israel to military victor

Luke 8:1–3 records that a group of women — Mary Magdalene, Joanna, Susanna, and "many others" — traveled with Jesus and the Twelve, providing financial support for his ministry from their own resources. This is a public, visible discipleship, not a hidden domestic role. At the Crucifixion, while most of the male disciples had fled, the women remained: Mary Magdalene, Mary the mother of James and Joseph, and the mother of the sons of Zebedee (Matthew 27:55–56). Their steadfastness at the Cross is a silent rebuke to the notion that women occupy only peripheral roles in salvation history.

Acts 21:9 records that Philip the Evangelist had "four unmarried daughters who prophesied." Paul's instruction in 1 Corinthians 11:5 — that women prophesying must cover their heads — presupposes that women's public prophecy in the congregation was normal and accepted. The question was not whether women could prophesy, but how to do so with appropriate reverence.

Justin Martyr and Irenaeus of Lyon both develop the parallel between Eve and Mary: as Eve's disobedience brought death, Mary's obedience brought life. This is a profoundly positive theological anthropology for women: the reversal of the Fall begins through a woman's free, obedient cooperation with God. Irenaeus of Lyon Against Heresies, Book V (c. AD 180) "As Eve was seduced by the word of an angel to flee from God, having rebelled against his Word, so Mary received the good tidings by means of the angel's word, so as to bear God, being obedient to his Word. And if the former did disobey God, yet the latter was persuaded to be obedient to God, in order that the Virgin Mary might become the patroness of the virg

VII. Female Saints and Martyrs in the OO Tradition The Oriental Orthodox calendar is filled with female saints — martyrs, monastics, equals-to-the-apostles, and teachers — whose lives the Church holds up as models of Christian perfection. St. Thecla of Iconium Equal to the Apostles · First Century Converted through Paul's preaching, Thecla renounced her betrothal, endured torture and attempted execution multiple times, and became an evangelist in her own right. Tertullian records that some used her example to justify women's right to teach and baptize — indicating her apostolic reputation was so strong it required theological adjudication. Venerated in Coptic, Syriac, and Armenian traditions. St. Mary Magdalene

1 Corinthians 14:34–35 "Women should remain silent in the churches. They are not allowed to speak, but must be in submission, as the law says. If they want to inquire about something, they should ask their own husbands at home; for it is disgraceful for a woman to speak in the church." Context: Chapter 14 is entirely about orderly conduct in the assembly — tongues, prophecy, and interpretation Key exegetical observations: Paul has already instructed women on how to prophesy publicly (11:5) — thus a total ban on speech cannot be what he intends here. The word for "speak" (laleō) in context likely refers to a specific disruptive kind of speech — most likely the practice of wives questioning or challenging their h

Jesus chose twelve male apostles. This was not a concession to cultural convention — Jesus routinely violated cultural convention in his treatment of women (see Section IV). The deliberate choice of male apostles, maintained by the Apostles in their appointment of successors (Acts 6; 1 Timothy 3; Titus 1), is held by the OO tradition to be a binding apostolic decision that the Church inherits, not one it can reverse. The Iconic Argument The priest at the altar acts in persona Christi — in the person of Christ. Christ, the eternal Son, took on a male human body in the Incarnation. The priest represents Christ the Bridegroom to his Bride, the Church (Ephesians 5:25–32). This is not a statement about women's infer

secs.h: Patristic Evidence for the Deaconess Office
secs.x: The office of deaconess is attested across multiple early sources: Didascalia Apostolorum (c. AD 230): Describes deaconesses as necessary for the baptism of adult women, for visiting sick women in pagan households, and for ministering to women in the congregation. Apostolic Constitutions (c. AD 380): Contains an ordination prayer for deaconesses, indicating a liturgical rite. Council of Nicaea (AD 325) : Canon 19 mentions deaconesses, indicating they were recognized at the highest level of Church authority. Pliny the Younger (c. AD 112): In his letter to Trajan about Christians in Bithynia, he mentions torturing "two female slaves who were called deaconesses" — evidence of the office in Asia Minor within living

XI. Objections Addressed ⚡ "Galatians 3:28 abolishes all gender distinctions in the Church." OO Response: Galatians 3:28 ("neither male nor female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus") is a statement about equal standing before God in terms of salvation, adoption, and the reception of the Spirit — not a statement that all functional distinctions cease to exist. Paul wrote Galatians 3:28 and 1 Corinthians 14:34 and 1 Timothy 2:12 — if his intent was to abolish all gender-based distinctions in church practice, he contradicted himself in the same letter collection. The OO tradition reads Galatians 3:28 as an affirmation of equal dignity and equal access to grace, not the abolition of all distinctions of role or f

XII. Frequently Asked Questions Can women preach or teach in the Oriental Orthodox Church? Yes — in a variety of contexts. Women teach in Sunday schools, catechetical programs, and church education at all levels. Female theologians write and lecture. Abbesses preach to their communities. The restriction that the Fathers and canonical tradition maintain is specifically on the ordained liturgical teaching office — preaching from the ambo at the Divine Liturgy as an ordained presbyter. Outside that specific context, women's teaching ministry has always been part of church life and is explicitly encouraged (Titus 2:3–4; Proverbs 31:26). Why is the Theotokos venerated so highly if women are subordinate in the Church

Every mainstream English Bible is translated from the same body of ancient manuscripts — Hebrew and Greek for the Old Testament (with the Church favouring the Greek Septuagint), and Greek for the New Testament. A translation is simply the act of carrying that fixed, inspired message from one language into another. The words on the English page differ; the revelation behind them is one. Think of it the way the Church has always thought of the Gospel itself: one truth proclaimed in many tongues. At Pentecost the Apostles spoke and "each one heard them speaking in his own language" (Acts 2:6) — the same message, many languages, no contradiction. The Coptic Church has read the scriptures in Greek, Coptic, Arabic, Ge'ez and now English for two thousand years without ever supposing it had more than one Bible. A helpful test: open any three reputable translations side by side at John 1:1. Every one of them says the Word was God, was in the beginning, and was with God. The vocabulary shifts — word order, punctuation, a synonym here or there — but the doctrine is identical. Translations vary in wording , not in the faith they deliver . So Why Do Any Differences Exist At All? Three honest reasons, none of them sinister. First, English changes: "thee" and "thou" were plain speech in 1611 and are archaic now, so revisions modernise the language. Second, translators must choose how literally to render idioms and figures of speech — this is the real dividing line, covered in the next section. Third, translators occasionally weigh manuscript evidence differently for a handful of verses. These are matters of craft and clarity, not competing revelations. "The grass withers, the flower fades, but the word of our God will stand forever." Isaiah 40:8

Almost every difference you will ever notice between two Bibles comes down to how literally the translators chose to work . There are three broad approaches, and knowing them lets you read any version wisely. Word-for-Word Formal Equivalence KJV · NKJV · ESV NASB · RSV Stays close to the original Thought-for-Thought Dynamic Equivalence NIV · NAB · NJB NEB · REB Prioritises readability Paraphrase Free Retelling Living Bible · The Message Good News (GNT) Loosest — use with caution

No English translation is perfect, and none is "forbidden." But some are markedly more reliable than others for an Orthodox Christian who wants both accuracy and a text compatible with the Church's Tradition. The table below rates common versions on three things that matter: how faithful (literal) they are, how readable they are, and whether they make a good main study Bible. Translation Approach Faithful Readable Good Main Bible? Recommended — Faithful & Church-compatible Orthodox Study Bible Word-for-word (LXX + NKJV) High Good Best NKJV (New King James) Word-for-word High Good Yes KJV (King James) Word-for-word High Archaic Yes NASB (New American Standard) Word-for-word Very High Stiff Yes Usable with care ESV (English Standard) Word-for-word High Good Care RSV (Revised Standard) Word-for-word High Good Care NIV (New International) Thought-for-thought Medium Very Good Care Not for study or doctrine Good News / GNT Thought-for-thought / free Low Very Good No Living Bible Paraphrase Low Very Good No The Message Paraphrase Very Low Very Good No New World Translation Sectarian (JW) Corrupted — Avoid Yes / High reliable | Care good but watch specific verses | Var. mixed | No not for serious study A Word on the "Bad" End Paraphrases such as The Message are not evil — they are simply one man's commentary dressed as scripture, and they should never anchor doctrine. The one version to genuinely avoid is the Jehovah's Witnesses' New World Translation, which deliberately alters key verses (most infamously John 1:1, rendered "the Word was a god") to deny the divinity of Christ. That is not a translation choice but a doctrinal corruption. See our page on the Divinity of Christ . A Closer Look at the NIV The New International Version deserves its own section because it is the most popular modern Bible in the English-speaking world, and because its strengths and weaknesses are widely misunderstood. A fair Orthodox assessment is this: the NIV is fine for children and new readers, but it muddies things once you dig into the details. This is the view held across many — if not all — of the Oriental Orthodox churches, and it holds up under examination. What the NIV Does Well The NIV reads clearly and naturally. For a child, a catechumen, or anyone reading Scripture for the first time, its plain English removes needless obstacles, and it is far more accurate than any paraphrase. As a first Bible or a reading Bible, it does real good. Where It Muddies the Details Because the NIV is a thought-for-thought translation, its committee frequently decides for the reader what an ambiguous verse means, and then prints only that meaning. Three examples that matter to Orthodox doctrine: 1. Holy Tradition (2 Thessalonians 2:15). Paul tells the Thessalonians to hold fast to what they were taught "by word" and "by letter." The Greek is paradosis — "that which is handed down," traditionally rendered "traditions." The NIV renders it "teachings," a softer word that blunts a verse plainly supporting the Church's Holy Tradition alongside Scripture. 2. Flesh vs. "sinful nature" (Romans 8, Galatians 5). The Greek sarx ("flesh") carries a rich range of meaning the Fathers explored deeply. Older NIV editions repeatedly replaced "flesh" with the interpretive phrase "sinful nature," committing the text to one theological reading and obscuring the patristic understanding. (The 2011 revision restored "flesh" in many places — itself an admission that the earlier choice had been an interpretation, not a translation.) 3. Readability over precision. In smoothing the text for easy reading, the NIV frequently resolves a deliberate ambiguity into a single sense. That is a gift to a beginner and a hazard to anyone building doctrine on the exact words. The pattern: none of these is a "lie" — each is a defensible interpretive choice. But that is exactly the problem. A study Bible should hand you the text and let the Church interpret; the NIV often hands you the interpretation and hides the text. Fine for a child; not what you want when the details matter. "So then, brethren, stand fast and hold the traditions which you were taught, whether by word or our epistle." 2 Thessalonians 2:15 (NKJV) The practical counsel that follows is simple: keep the NIV, if you like it, as a readable companion — but study, memorise doctrine, and settle arguments from a word-for-word Bible. What the Coptic Orthodox Church Recommends The Coptic Orthodox Church has never bound the faithful to a single English translation the way it holds to the Greek Septuagint and the Coptic texts for its liturgy. But its guidance to English-speaking Copts has been consistent and clear. Pope Shenouda III of Alexandria 117th Pope · 1971–2012 Under Pope Shenouda III the Coptic Church consistently pointed English-speaking faithful toward the New King James Version as the most suitable everyday English Bible: faithful and word-for-word, based on the traditional Byzantine / Received Text underlying the Church's New Testament, yet readable in modern English. Coptic parishes and Sunday-school materials across the English-speaking diaspora overwhelmingly use the NKJV for exactly this reason.

secs.h: Why the Septuagint Matters for the Old Testament
secs.x: Choosing a translation is not only about English style; for the Old Testament it is also about which ancient text is being translated. Here the Orthodox position is distinctive and important. The Church's Old Testament is the Septuagint (LXX), the Greek translation used by the Apostles themselves, not the later Hebrew Masoretic text that most Protestant Bibles follow. This is not a minor preference. When the New Testament quotes the Old Testament — roughly 300 times — it is almost always quoting the Septuagint. The most famous case is the prophecy of the Virgin Birth. Isaiah 7:14 — "Virgin" or "Young Woman"? The Septuagint renders Isaiah 7:14 with parthenos — "virgin." Matthew 1:23 quotes it in exactly that form to announce the birth of Christ. But some modern translations, following the Hebrew almah and modern critical fashion, render it "young woman." The RSV famously did this and caused an uproar; a young-woman reading quietly weakens the Messianic prophecy that the Gospel itself depends on. A translation that keeps "virgin" is standing with Matthew and the Church. "Therefore the Lord Himself will give you a sign: Behold, the virgin shall conceive and bear a Son, and shall call His name Immanuel." Isaiah 7:14 (LXX) / Matthew 1:23 This is why an Orthodox reader benefits from a Bible whose Old Testament rests on the Septuagint — above all the Orthodox Study Bible — and why, among ordinary translations, the ones that preserve the traditional readings (KJV, NKJV) are preferred. For the full story see our page on the Biblical Canon . Scripture Came Through the Church — Why Tradition Matters Once you understand how the Bible was translated, copied and handed down, a deeper point comes into view — one that is among the most useful things an Orthodox Christian can raise with a Protestant friend. The Bible did not drop from heaven as a finished, bound book. It came to us through the Church , and that fact quietly undoes the idea that Scripture can stand entirely alone, apart from Tradition. The Church Came First For the first decades of Christianity there was no New Testament at all. The Apostles preached, taught and celebrated the Eucharist; the faith was living and complete before a single Gospel was written. When the New Testament letters and Gospels were finally set down, they were written within the Church, by her members, to her congregations — not handed to outsiders as a rulebook. The community existed first; the text was born inside it. The Bible's Own Table of Contents Is Tradition Here is the argument that stops many a discussion: nowhere does the Bible list its own books. No verse names the twenty-seven books of the New Testament. The canon — the very decision about which writings are Scripture and which are not — was discerned and settled by the Church over several centuries, through her bishops, councils and Fathers. So to hold the Bible at all is already to trust a decision of Holy Tradition. A Protestant who says "the Bible alone" is relying, for the contents of that Bible, on the very Church authority he means to set aside. Sola scriptura cannot even tell you what "scriptura" is. Scripture Itself Commands Holding to Tradition Far from opposing Tradition, the New Testament explicitly commands it. St. Paul tells the faithful to hold fast the traditions they were taught "by word" as well as "by epistle," and praises those who keep them. The written word never claims to be the whole of the deposit; John says as much when he notes that not everything Christ did was written down (John 21:25). "Now I praise you, brethren, that you remember me in all things and keep the traditions just as I delivered them to you." 1 Corinthians 11:2 (NKJV) "Therefore, brethren, stand fast and hold the traditions which you were taught, whether by word or our epistle." 2 Thessalonians 2:15 (NKJV) St. Basil the Great c. 330–379 AD In his work On the Holy Spirit , Basil teaches that the Church holds her faith from two sources — the written Scriptures and the unwritten Tradition handed down from the Apostles — and that both carry the same force for true religion. Practices as basic as facing east to pray, the words of the Eucharistic blessing, and the sign of the cross, he observes, are nowhere written in Scripture but come from Apostolic Tradition. The Church never lived by the text alone. St. Irenaeus of Lyon c. 130–202 AD Writing against the Gnostics, Irenaeus argued that the true faith is guarded in the Church through the unbroken succession of bishops from the Apostles. He pointed to whole nations of believers who held the faith rightly by Tradition, "without paper and ink" — proof that the Gospel lived in the Church's teaching before, and beneath, the written page. ( Against Heresies 3.4)

secs.h: The King James Version — A History, and Why It Is Reliable
secs.x: The King James Version of 1611 is one of the most influential books in the English language — and one of the most misunderstood. A claim heard often today runs like this: because King James I was a man, and a king, the Bible he commissioned was slanted to make women submit and subjects obey. It is a memorable charge. It is also historically false, and the record is public and checkable. Here is what actually happened. How the KJV Was Made King James authorised the translation at the Hampton Court Conference in 1604, and it was published in 1611. The work was done not by one man but by roughly 47 scholars organised into six committees — two each at Westminster, Oxford and Cambridge. They translated from the Hebrew of the Old Testament and the Greek Received Text of the New, and they leaned heavily on the earlier English Bibles, above all the work of William Tyndale. Each company's draft was reviewed by the others and by a final revising committee. The whole structure was built to make sure no single person — least of all the king — could quietly insert his own agenda. It Was a Revision, Not a Royal Invention The KJV was not composed from scratch in 1611. Scholars estimate that roughly 80 to 90 percent of its New Testament wording is taken directly from William Tyndale , whose translation appeared in 1526 and who was strangled and burned for it in 1536 — decades before James was even born. The English Bible the KJV gives us is essentially Tyndale's, polished through three generations of revisers (the Great Bible, the Geneva Bible, the Bishops' Bible). The supposedly “biased” passages were already fixed in English long before James, and they stood in Latin, Greek and Syriac for more than a thousand years before that. What James Actually Cared About James did give the translators rules, drawn up by Archbishop Richard Bancroft. His one genuine political concern was marginal notes . The Geneva Bible, beloved of the Puritans, carried study notes, some of which questioned the authority of kings — one famous note on Exodus praised the Hebrew midwives for disobeying Pharaoh. James considered such notes seditious. So Bancroft's sixth rule forbade doctrinal margin notes, permitting only brief explanations of Hebrew and Greek words. His other preferences were matters of church government , not sex: keep the old church vocabulary, render the Greek as “church” rather than “congregation,” “bishop” rather than “elder.” Nowhere in the rules, the translators' records, or the finished text is there a single instruction touching the status of women. That part of the story is simply an anachronism. Why this makes the KJV reliable: its trustworthiness rests on public, verifiable history — a large committee that checked one another, a known Hebrew and Greek textual base, a documented chain of descent back to Tyndale and the ancient manuscripts, and a set of royal rules whose one political edge concerned anti-monarchy footnotes, not women. The KJV is a faithful English rendering of the same Scriptures the Church has always read. That is precisely why the Coptic Church is at home with its careful modern descendant, the NKJV.

secs.h: How to Choose a Bible Translation
secs.x: Put the pieces together and the decision becomes simple. Start with your purpose, then pick accordingly. 1. Decide what the Bible is for For study, doctrine and liturgy , choose a word-for-word translation — the Orthodox Study Bible or the NKJV first of all. For a young child or a brand-new reader , a readable version such as the NIV is a reasonable starting point, with the plan of moving to a fuller translation as they grow. For devotional reading aloud , readability matters more, but never let a paraphrase be someone's only Bible. 2. Prefer a text the Church can stand behind Favour translations whose Old Testament respects the Septuagint readings and whose New Testament rests on the traditional Received Text — the NKJV and KJV both do. The Orthodox Study Bible does this most completely and adds the deuterocanonical books the Church reads. 3. Match the reader to the version An honest, practical ranking for an Orthodox home: keep the Orthodox Study Bible as the anchor; use the NKJV as the everyday text (this is the Coptic Church's counsel); let a child begin with a clear version like the NIV and grow into the NKJV; treat paraphrases as devotional extras, never as the main Bible; and avoid the New World Translation entirely. 4. Read it within the Church, not alone The deepest safeguard is not the translation but the community. Scripture was written in the Church, canonised by the Church, and is rightly read within the Church's Tradition and the mind of the Fathers. No translation, however good, is a substitute for reading with the Church. See Scripture & Tradition . The one-line answer: get the Orthodox Study Bible if you can; otherwise the NKJV. Use the NIV for children as a first step. Skip the paraphrases for study, and avoid the New World Translation altogether.

Is it a sin to read the NIV? No. The NIV is a real translation of Scripture, not a corruption, and reading it is no sin. The counsel is simply to prefer a word-for-word Bible for study and doctrine, because the NIV's thought-for-thought method sometimes settles interpretive questions for you. Is the King James Version the only true Bible? No. The "KJV-only" position is a modern Protestant idea the Orthodox Church does not hold. The KJV is an excellent, faithful translation resting on the traditional text, and Orthodox readers love it — but the NKJV carries the same textual tradition in modern English, and the Church has never taught that salvation depends on one English version. Which Bible should I buy first? If you can get one Bible, get the Orthodox Study Bible: Septuagint Old Testament, NKJV New Testament, the deuterocanonical books, and notes from the Fathers. If you want a plain, affordable everyday Bible, the NKJV. Why do Orthodox Bibles have more books? Because the Church's Old Testament is the Septuagint, which always contained the deuterocanonical books — Tobit, Judith, Wisdom, Sirach, and others. These were read as Scripture from the beginning and only removed from Protestant Bibles in the 16th century. The full account is on our Biblical Canon page. Are modern translations trying to change Christianity? Mostly no. Most differences are honest questions of English style and manuscript weighing. A few choices — "young woman" for "virgin," "teachings" for "traditions" — do carry doctrinal weight, which is exactly why an Orthodox reader keeps a faithful, word-for-word Bible as the main text and reads within the Church.
